author: @cherylm2C6671
text: This looks like where metal 3 D printing came from!

author: @74Persia
text:
if those Cu NP s are protected with long polymers you may add some form of small molecule electrolyte in the solution in the
process of ink drying there is  a point which the density of electrolyte ions becomes enough to guard NP surface against polymer attachment and both become mostly washable afterwards . from energy point of view its like melting snow by infrared radiation or by  adding salt (hardly similar !)  given that the NP were protected against oxidation this should result in instant conductivity  .one other advantage is that the polymer will detach before drying out when the NPs scan still move to arrange.on the other hand using flash will cause shrinking by polymer loss that will be opposed by surface tension? which might cause cracks when the particle sizes variance is high these cracks works like captive load.if not disconnection. these are just my understanding the underlying actor might be the moisture in the air acting with the remaining salt but its not very likely .I thought to share anyway

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: thanks for the share mate - good info

replies.author: @74Persia
replies.text: I think your videos have more potential commercial values than any end product you might sell on the side.Great work sir

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text:
74Persia
I am not quite sure what you mean mate

replies.author: @74Persia
replies.text: Robert Murray-Smith once we needed a few educational video for some chemical process to be made for a presentation . they charged us nearly $3000 for a few short videos which finally we decided not to use.them because of poor English translation and very dirty lab equipment and tools used among many other problems . and it took them very long time to finish the useless work seeing your videos I was thinking that this was what I had in mind .you seem to make these effortlessly and just said that I think your work is great. thats all

author: @vicebi
text: Perdona en México también le llamamos condensador a un Capacitor, claro de forma común, pero lo siento; Capacitor quise decir... Vicente.

author: @vicebi
text: Gentil Robet, disfuto mucho de tu trabajo y experimento y agradezco que los compartas. Sabes mi ingles no es tan bueno como tu español, pero sin importar eso, me a gustado y interesado lo hecho con Cobre "cooper" y con Graphen... que interesante idea la del condesador... un saludo desde Mexico.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: hola, gracias por sus amables palabras. No entiendo lo que quieres decir con condensador. Puede explicar más. - Rob
